data.title: Ant, JD.com, and others are competing for stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong, with requirements comparable to banks.
data.description:
Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance will come into effect on August 1st. On the afternoon of July 29th, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) disclosed its regulatory framework for stablecoin licenses and released a series of supporting documents. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority stated that licensing will be an ongoing process, and institutions interested in obtaining a license earlier should submit their applications to the HKMA before September 30, 2025. The HKMA has consistently emphasized the prohibition of speculation. At the meeting, the HKMA also emphasized that, as of today, it has not issued any licenses, and that falsely claiming to be a licensee or applicant is illegal. With regulatory details finalized and applications for stablecoin licenses imminent, many securities firms have been quick to offer their interpretations, covering key points such as the application timeline, licensing requirements, and speculation about the issuing authority. Industry insiders anticipate that the initial batch of licenses will be very small, jokingly claiming that "the application threshold will be as difficult as that of banks." The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) has announced that the licensing system comprises four documents: the consultation conclusions and guidelines for the "Guidelines on the Supervision of Licensed Stablecoin Issuers," the consultation conclusions and guidelines for the "Guidelines on Combating Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing (Licensed Stablecoin Issuers)," the "Summary Notes on the Licensing Regime for Stablecoin Issuers," and the "Summary Notes on Transitional Provisions for Existing Stablecoin Issuers," which relate to the licensing system and application procedures. The two sets of guidelines will take effect on August 1, and market participants should comply with them. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority stated that it encourages institutions interested in applying for licenses to seize communication opportunities and contact the HKMA through the designated email address on or before August 31, 2025. This move is intended to enable applicants to understand regulatory expectations in a timely manner and obtain targeted feedback, so as to better improve their application materials and improve application efficiency. The regulator emphasized that the issuance of stablecoin licenses will be an ongoing process, but for those institutions that are well prepared and hope to obtain approval as soon as possible, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority has set the first important time node: September 30, 2025. Institutions interested in being considered in this batch must submit an application to the HKMA on or before that date. However, public information shows that HKMA Deputy Chief Executive Chen Weimin explicitly stated that September 30th is not the final deadline, and that institutions not included in the first batch still have a chance. The first batch of licenses is expected to be announced in early 2026. Qualifications for stablecoin issuers are "comparable to those of banks." Analysts including Liu Xinqi's team at Cathay Pacific and Haitong's non-bank lending division and the financial team at Guosen Securities' Economic Research Institute reviewed the four documents and provided interpretations of requirements related to applicant qualifications, reserve asset requirements, anti-money laundering measures, and the transition period for existing issuers.

First, in terms of applicant qualifications, the applicant must be a registered corporation or authorized institution in Hong Kong, China, and meet the financial requirements of not less than HK$25 million in paid-up share capital or another currency freely convertible into Hong Kong dollars.

Second, in terms of reserve asset requirements, licensees should establish policies and procedures to ensure full backing by reserve assets. Reserve assets include cash, bank deposits with a term of not more than three months, claims on or highly liquid bonds guaranteed by governments, central banks, etc. with a term of not more than one year, or overnight reverse repurchase agreements backed by these. Reserve assets must be denominated in a specified stablecoin reference currency. If other currencies are used as reserve assets, the prior written approval of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority is required.

Third, in terms of anti-money laundering, issuers must continuously monitor transactions in customer wallets (including custodial and non-custodial wallets) and the identities of holders. At the same time, additional risk controls must be implemented for non-custodial wallet transactions to strictly prevent money laundering and terrorist financing risks.

Fourth, in terms of stablecoin types and segregation, the reserve asset pool for each designated stablecoin must be consistent with the reference asset of that type of stablecoin; the reserve assets of each type of stablecoin must be segregated from other reserve pools; and effective trust arrangements must be implemented to ensure that the reserve assets are independent of the issuer. Fifth, regarding information disclosure, issuers must prepare daily statements on the face value of stablecoins in circulation and the market value and composition of their reserve assets, and submit these reports to the HKMA weekly. These reports must be certified by an external auditor, including a report on the market value and composition of their reserve assets, the face value of stablecoins in circulation, and whether their reserve assets adequately support the face value of their stablecoins in circulation. These reports must be disclosed within one month of the end of the period. Sixth, regarding the transition period for existing issuers, the "Summary of Transition Provisions for Existing Stablecoin Issuers" primarily establishes a six-month transition period to process institutions that previously conducted stablecoin issuance business in Hong Kong and grant them temporary licenses. Existing issuers interested in applying for a license should apply by October 31, 2025 at the latest. Issuers should actively apply for a formal issuance license. If their application is unsuccessful within three months of the effective date of the Ordinance, they must orderly wind down their Hong Kong operations within four months. It is expected that only a single-digit number of licenses will be approved in the first round, leading to widespread speculation within the industry. Regarding the number of licenses to be issued, the Hong Kong government has long made a decision, adopting a cautious approach to the qualifications of stablecoin issuers. The number of stablecoin licenses issued in the first round will be strictly limited to the single digit. Eddie Yue, Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority, previously stated that "at most, only a few stablecoin licenses will be granted in the initial stage."

Hong Kong SAR Government Financial Services and the Treasury Bureau Director Paul Chan previously stated that, taking into account the risks involved in stablecoins as a relatively new product, as well as considerations for user protection and market carrying capacity, the threshold for issuing licenses is quite high. Stablecoin issuers that obtain licenses must have practical application scenarios and operate their businesses in a robust and sustainable manner. It is reported that cross-border trade has become one of the important application scenarios.

In fact, dozens of institutions have previously proactively contacted the HKMA team, and some have explicitly expressed their intention to apply for stablecoin licenses.

As early as March 2023, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority launched the "Stablecoin Issuer Sandbox Project", which aims to provide a controlled testing environment for institutions intending to issue stablecoins, allowing them to verify their business models and technical systems under regulatory exemptions.

In July of the same year, the first batch of stablecoin issuers in the Sandbox were announced. JD.com Group's JD Coin Chain Technology (Hong Kong) and Yuanbi Innovation Technology applied, and Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong), Anmi Group, and Hong Kong Telecom (HKT) chose to apply jointly. The five institutions involved include two blockchain technology companies: JD.com's JD CoinChain Technology (Hong Kong) and Yuanbi Innovation Technology, both subsidiaries of JD.com. The other three, Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong), Ansai Group, and Hong Kong Telecom (HKT), chose to jointly apply. In February 2025, Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong), Ansai Group, and HKT jointly announced that they had reached an agreement to establish a joint venture and hoped to apply for a license from the Hong Kong Monetary Authority to issue a stablecoin pegged to the Hong Kong dollar. In June of this year, news emerged that Ant Group and JD.com had both applied for stablecoin licenses. Previously, Ant Group stated that its international business unit was planning to apply for stablecoin licenses in Singapore and Hong Kong. Singapore-based Ant International plans to apply for a stablecoin issuance license immediately after Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance takes effect in August. On June 17, JD.com Chairman Richard Liu stated that JD.com hopes to apply for stablecoin licenses in all major currency countries. Using these licenses, JD.com will enable global business transactions, reducing global cross-border payment costs by 90% and increasing efficiency to under 10 seconds. In his article "The Steady Progress of Stablecoins," Eddie Yue, Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority, noted that "at best, only a few stablecoin licenses will be granted in the initial stages." We believe that the few issuance licenses available will be prioritized for companies with relevant application scenarios, such as cross-border payments. However, regulators have also emphasized that early entry into the "sandbox" does not guarantee license approval. Why are stablecoin issuance and trading licenses so important? Industry insiders believe that the implementation of Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance and the US's Genius Act, both of which are advancing stablecoin legislation, signal the regulatory acceptance of stablecoins by the global mainstream financial system. As a new digital stage in the evolution of currency, stablecoins are and will profoundly reshape the paradigm of global cross-border payments, trade settlement, and asset allocation. Huaxi Securities believes that the inclusion of stablecoins and other virtual assets into the traditional financial system could bring about three changes. First, licensed platforms or service providers will become mainstream, and choosing regulated trading platforms and stablecoin assets will reduce risk. Second, the on-chain transfer of real assets such as RWAs will become a regulatory focus. Third, licenses will become a key factor supporting valuations, and leading companies holding core licenses will have first-mover advantages and competitive advantages. Regarding who will be the first to obtain stablecoin issuance licenses, Ant Group and JD.com are both favored. In the trading sector, several Chinese securities firms have already secured entry. According to incomplete statistics, on June 25th, Guotai Junan International announced that it had obtained a full license for virtual asset trading in Hong Kong, attracting significant market attention and positive feedback. That same month, Hafu Securities, a subsidiary of East Fortune, received approval from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission to provide virtual asset trading services. Tianfeng Securities announced that its wholly-owned subsidiary, Tianfeng International Securities, had also received approval from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission to provide virtual asset trading services through omnibus account arrangements. In July, CMB International Securities announced that it had received licenses from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission for virtual asset trading services. The Founder Financial team believes that stablecoins have the potential to become disruptors in the international payment system, and that piloting stablecoins in Hong Kong is of great strategic significance. They are firmly optimistic about investment opportunities in the cryptocurrency industry chain.

data.title: Samourai Co-founders Lost Their Legal Battle With The DOJ, Pleads Guilty to Unlicensed Money Transmission Charges
data.description:
Samourai Wallet Co-Founders Set to Plead Guilty in Crypto Mixing Case

Keonne Rodriguez and William Lonergan Hill, the co-founders of privacy-focused Samourai Wallet, have finally given and plead guilty to their charges after a long grueling trial.

In a major win for DOJ, Samourai Wallet developers have plead guilty to unlicensed money transmission.

The charge had been heavily disputed, described as an unclear application of the law.

The developers face fines and up to five years in prison. Full story👇 pic.twitter.com/nNzpMQ4hHz

— The Rage (@theragetech) July 30, 2025

The pair submitted their filings to the New York federal court on Tuesday to what prosecutors are alleged to be an unlicensed money-transmitting business that was leveraged on by criminals to mask unlawful transactions.

When the pair first appeared on court in April 2024, they have denied any forms of wrongdoings.

The change in plea marks a dramatic turn in one of the highest-profile legal battles over cryptocurrency privacy tools in the U.S.

Facing A Potential 25-Year Sentence

Rodriguez, Samourai’s CEO, and Hill, the chief technology officer, were charged with conspiracy to commit money laundering, which carried a maximium prison sentence of 20 years.

Additionally, the pair were also charged with operating an unlicensed money-transmitting business, which adds an additional five years to the sentencing, bringing the maximum punishment to 25 years behind bars.

🚨 NEW: The Samourai developers - Keonne Rodriguez and William Lonergan Hill - have both pled guilty to count 2 of their indictment, the conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money transmitter business charge.

More details in the video below: pic.twitter.com/PZVpJtH9Pw

— Frank Corva (@frankcorva) July 30, 2025

Judge Denise Cote has scheduled hearings to discuss the updated pleas. Details on whether the guilty pleas will alter sentencing outcomes have not been disclosed. The trial was previously set for November 3.

Throughout the year, the co-founders vigorously contested the charges. In April, they sought to dismiss the case by stating a document from Deputy General Todd Blanche that said the DOJ won't prosecute crypto mixer operators for "unwitting violations of regulations."

2. The DOJ will not go after "virtual currency exchanges, mixing and tumbling services, and offline wallets for the acts of their end users or unwitting violations of regulations" and instead, DOJ "will pursue the illicit financing of [criminal enterprises] by the individuals and… pic.twitter.com/NXlSgVWHqo

— Amanda Tuminelli (@amandatums) April 8, 2025

This seemingly suggests that that pair was suggesting that they had no idea that their protocol was being used to mix illegal funds, but prosecutors weren't buying it.

Their lawyers later argued that the pair was also mislead by prosecutors who tricked them to believe that they didn't require a money transmitter license six months before they were eventually charged.

This attempt to to sway the prosecutor's case was also ineffective.

The Case’s Wider Impact: Privacy Tools and DeFi in the Crosshairs

Samourai Wallet, like other crypto mixers, operated by blending multiple users’ transactions to obscure origins—an approach now drawing sharp scrutiny from U.S. law enforcement.

👀 All eyes on the Tornado Cash trial!

With recent guilty pleas in the Samourai case, the landscape of crypto regulation is changing. What's next for #DeFi?

We had the pleasure of sitting down with @cryptonomista , Co-Founder & CSO of @hodlwithLedn. Stay tuned for the interview… pic.twitter.com/vQQ5yKUznR

— CoinDesk (@CoinDesk) July 30, 2025

The guilty pleas land as the crypto industry closely watches the ongoing trial of Roman Storm, co-creator of fellow mixing protocol Tornado Cash.

Privacy advocates warn that convictions in these cases could set legal precedents criminalizing open-source privacy technology and significantly curtailing digital rights in decentralized finance.

data.title: A must-read for project owners: Learn from Shi Yongxin how to market through group thinking
data.description:
Author: Jiayi Li Source: X, @jiayi_en

In 1981, 16-year-old Shi Yongxin entered the then-almost-forgotten Shaolin Temple. At the time, the temple housed only nine monks, struggling to survive by farming and receiving incense offerings. A turning point came a year later: the film "Shaolin Temple," starring Jet Li, became a nationwide sensation, catapulting the ancient temple into the public eye overnight.

Shi Yongxin accurately grasped this "mental dividend." He didn't invent kung fu, nor was he the most skilled martial artist, but he achieved a groundbreaking brand positioning: he embedded the notion of "Shaolin Temple = Chinese Kung Fu" in the minds of audiences worldwide. Over the next few decades, he systematically compiled martial arts classics, promoted performances overseas, engaged in cultural dissemination, and established commercial licensing agreements. Starting from a religious site, he transformed Shaolin into a global gateway to "Kung Fu awareness." More importantly, this awareness went beyond "cultural influence" and ultimately translated into real money: tickets, IP, real estate, intangible asset management...Awareness became the gateway to business.

This is the power of "crowd mind":When you leave a clear and unique label in the user's mind, you have the right to tell a story, set a price, and maintain a long-term presence.

How does crowd mind relate to Web3 projects?

You might ask: What can Web3 projects learn from a monk who built a Shaolin Temple brand for forty years? I'm showing you Shi Yongxin not because he understands live streaming or cultural IP, but because he's accomplished something almost all Web3 projects strive for but rarely achieve: establishing a defining keyword in the minds of global users. Web2 is about business, and of course, market share—that is, the user base within your vertical sector. This is because both valuation and the business itself in traditional businesses rely heavily on the product's immediate market competitiveness after launch. For Web3 projects, I personally believe that a project's "share of the public mind" far outweighs "actual market share." But "paying attention to the public mind" isn't just empty talk; it permeates every stage of a project's journey from zero to one, especially the crucial milestone of the TGE. After the TGE, with the arrival of liquidity, the project's operational logic completely changes. You're no longer just telling a story and attracting attention; you're now dealing with real market pricing, arbitrage, and game-playing. This shift is drastic. If you're not well prepared, all the initial excitement and anticipation can quickly vanish in a matter of days. Therefore, projects must consider in advance: Before the TGE, what kind of user mind should you capture? What narrative should you tell? Where should you position yourself in the user's mind? Let's discuss each of these in detail. Before the TGE, how should projects build "collective mind"? For most Web3 projects, the TGE is their first foray into the public market. But the true determinant of success or failure lies before the TGE. This phase offers a golden window for capturing the user's attention. It not only determines the success of your token launch, but also your ability to leverage this "collective attention moment" to plant a lasting, memorable label in the user's mind. How you use this time to clearly articulate your project's positioning, build trust, and maintain stable expectations will determine whether you can attract truly valuable early adopters. Otherwise, you might not just be waiting for a launch, but rather a demise. I usually recommend that projects that haven't yet launched a TGE conduct a self-examination using the "Three Mental Questions": 1. Which tier do users consider you? Are you a leading player in your field, or a fringe project? The underlying equation is a very practical one: User perception of your project's tier = expected value of your TGE = time spent following your project = actual performance data, etc. Your actual performance data and user engagement often reflect users' subjective perception of whether you're worth investing in. These aren't solely based on what you've done, but more on "where you appear to be in the tier." 2. What exactly do users remember about you? This is probably the most common overestimation of Web3 entrepreneurs. Many teams present their projects with rigorous logic and clear structure, but after listening for twenty minutes, I still ask, "So, what's your breaking point?" The reality is harsh. In this extremely fragmented market, with countless project announcements every day, don't expect users to truly understand you. They'll only remember a few key words that evoke associations and emotions. Therefore, you must refine your content, ultimately distilling it down to three key takeaways for users: memorable, inspiring money-making potential, and relevant to future explosive growth potential.

Speaking human language is the skill most projects lack.

3. Can group trust be maintained?

How can you build a project that is trusted by users? This is the most easily overlooked point, and also the most vulnerable layer.

Even if you have strong technical skills and a great storyteller, once users question your personality, team, or behavior patterns, trust collapses, and the mind automatically loses its anchor.

Trust often collapses not because of major events, but rather the accumulation of seemingly insignificant little things. For example, a user might raise a question but receive no response, even after repeated inquiries. A reward payment, agreed upon, is repeatedly delayed without even an explanation. Someone in the community begins to question the plan, but the team plays deadlock or coldly says, "We'll discuss this internally." Sometimes, while the project may appear to be well-reasoned from the outside, rumors circulate behind the scenes that it's just an arbitrage scheme. Each of these incidents may seem minor, but it's this sense of "saying one thing, doing another" that gradually erodes users' initial trust, especially those who were your earliest supporters. They were once your most valuable assets, those who truly believed in your story. But once that trust cracks, they're the first to leave and the last to return. Just as when people around the world mention Chinese Kung Fu, most people's first thought isn't Wing Chun, Bajiquan, or Tai Chi, but Shaolin. Wing Chun isn't bad, but it hasn't had its own Shi Yongxin. You need to be the one who builds the collective mindset for the project. After the TGE, the project officially entered the realm of a "financial target." After the TGE, the project is no longer just about products, visions, and stories; it becomes a financial asset with a price, liquidity, and secondary trading. Your value, worth buying, and potential for growth begin to be verified in the most public and transparent way. The first thing to change is the user demographic. Those early users who once shared your ideals, ran testnets, and actively participated in the community have also undergone a transformation. They are now both users and traders. A larger wave of traders is just now entering the market. They aren't here to "listen to your story" but to ask a more direct question: "Does your coin offer any profit opportunities?" Few Web3 products are truly irreplaceable. Even if you're 20% or 30% better than your competitors, if the price doesn't move and the market doesn't fluctuate, you'll still be quickly abandoned. Users won't give you the time and patience to grow; they'll immediately chase the project that "seems more promising." Therefore, projects must directly answer the question: Why should others buy your coin? This reflects three typical user mental models: Low-level players: My product is great. Users: It doesn't matter whether it's good or not; I'm not afraid to buy. The most common mentality among these projects is: "We have leading technology, a great product experience, and a dedicated team." But the market won't reward your hard work. The user's reaction is usually, "No matter how good you say it is, will there be volatility? No? Then I won't buy it." This is a classic example of a "divergence between product value and financial value." In Web3, products alone, without price elasticity, cannot sustain user trust. You can be a builder, but in the eyes of users, you're just a "coin with no expectations." The reality is that product experience is no longer a scarce commodity, but price expectations that capture attention are. So you need to understand: you think you're building a product, but in reality, you're competing for access to financial sentiment. Mid-level players: I see good news, so I pump the market. Users: Speculate in the short term, and then run for the profit. The vast majority of Web3 users are short-term speculators. They don't expect to contribute to the long-term, but as long as you pump the market, have a good rhythm, and there are good news, they will join in. They are not believers, nor are they community evangelists. But as long as you create tradability, they will enter the market for a round of trading. This isn't a bad thing. On the contrary, it means you're making a splash. Users know you're a project with a scalping potential, and even if they can't hold onto it, it's worth investing in. As long as you can successfully pump the market a few times, the market will begin to recognize you as a coin with a "market potential." Your token will be added to users' watchlists, and a group of people will be waiting for your next move. From no one paying attention to participation to a stakeholder—this is how the "price elasticity mindset" in Web3 is slowly established. Advanced players: Make users feel "this coin is worth holding on to; if you sell it, you won't be able to get back on board." The ideal, yet most difficult, user mindset is for users to actively hold onto your coins when they're liquidating their holdings. What comes to their mind is not “can I make quick money”, but “this project, I may still need it in the next round.” “This coin, once it goes up in price, I may not be able to buy it back.” To reach this level, a project must establish a complete “trust × expectation × feedback” cycle and meet at least four conditions: · The long-term direction of the project is clear, and the narrative does not jump back and forth; · The product progress is rhythmic, and users can see hope; · The project party has favorable news, and the coin price is not weak. · The coin price is resilient, forming an emotional resilience that “there is still room for discussion if it goes up, and it can be pulled up again if it falls”; This type of token doesn't necessarily skyrocket daily, but users know, "You're an asset worth investing in for the long term," and they'll naturally hold on to it, spread the word, and maintain it. SUI: A Real-World Example of a Mental Reversal Take a coin I recently listed as a long-term bet: $SUI. Let's break it down. SUI boasts a stellar team (this time, the R&D team behind Facebook's meta project), and with a multi-billion dollar primary market valuation, it's become a source of FOMO for major investment institutions. Honestly, I didn't think SUI performed well during the initial TGE. The community generally felt the project was arrogant and disconnected from the community. It wasn't until a year and a half ago that SUI suddenly realized the importance of community, continuing to promote its ecosystem while also focusing on community development. I won't go into details about the secondary aspects due to regulatory issues. You all know what happened after that. Suddenly, SUI became a "little SOL" in the market's mindset, joining the list of assets users are willing to hold long-term. Sui had already experienced two events this summer that tested market confidence: first, the security incident at the end of May involving the ecosystem project Cetus, which drained its approximately $223 million liquidity pool; and second, the massive token unlock in early July, worth nearly $200 million, one of the largest releases of the entire quarter. Normally, this series of negative developments should have led to a price crash and a breakdown in community sentiment. Instead, SUI not only remained resistant to market abandonment, but actually soared to $4.39 the day before yesterday, reaching a new high since February and becoming one of the most actively traded projects in the sector. Why did it hold up? The key isn't simply that the Sui team didn't shy away from negative events like the hack, but rather quickly took responsibility. What's truly significant is that over the past year or so, Sui has slowly transformed user perceptions, transforming its once-criticized image of "arrogance and aloofness" into a project worthy of trust and long-term investment. Take the example of the hack of the ecosystem project Cetus. While the attack was caused by a third-party smart contract, Sui wasn't directly responsible. The team didn't shirk responsibility. They immediately suspended the relevant contracts, froze two affected wallets, and collaborated with Sui validators to initiate a vote. They also partnered with the Sui Foundation to arrange a loan to raise compensation funds to guarantee full compensation to the victims. Ultimately, 90.9% of validators voted in favor of releasing $162 million in frozen assets, and the compensation plan was successfully approved. The entire process was transparent, swift, and highly effective, demonstrating once again that this team was resilient and willing to provide the necessary support at a critical moment. What it demonstrates is this: As long as you establish a clear mental anchor early on and consistently deliver on it after the TGE, the market will give you time and space. Trust is the only thing I'm willing to bet on. Many projects approach me for marketing, but I've only collaborated on a few. It's not because I have high standards, but because I only invest my time and reputation in trustworthy teams. Before I decide whether to assist, I always complete a project's design and development. My core criteria are: Is the team trustworthy? And does its community trust them? If any of these criteria are inconsistent, no matter how compelling the narrative, I won't partner. I don't believe I can improve a project through marketing alone, and I certainly won't place my trust in an irresponsible team. Ultimately, the core competitiveness of Web3 projects isn't about technological barriers or funding. It's about whether you can create a clear, credible, and relatable place in the hearts of a group of people. This is the collective mind, and it's the true deciding factor in Web3.
